turns.content: I'm trying to build a DSL in my new Rust job inspired by sql!{} and html!{} macros, but I keep getting errors. I tried writing this function that takes &TokenStream and returns () with #[proc_macro] attached to it.

You need to understand that a proc-macro isn't just any Rust function; it must implement the ProcMacro trait from the proc-macro crate. Your function signature needs to match exactly what the compiler expects when you write #[proc_macro] on a module or item.
Oh okay, so I should just add that attribute? But my code still fails with 'cannot find symbol' errors. I thought any function with #[proc_macro] would automatically become a macro like sql!{}.

The issue is that #[proc_macro] must be placed on the actual module or item containing your logic, not just on a standalone function. You need to create a lib.rs file with impl ProcMacro for your proc-macro crate so the compiler knows how to invoke it.
Wait, do I need to implement something else? What exactly does the trait require?

You must implement one of the three traits: ProcMacro2018 for Rust 2018 edition, or more commonly now, the function-like proc-macro which implements a custom derive macro. For your DSL use case, you'll likely want to parse tokens using syn::parse::<YourStruct>(tokens) and then emit new tokens back with TokenStream.
That makes sense! But I'm still confused about how the token stream flows. Can someone explain exactly what happens when I write sql!{}?

When you write sql!{}, the macro expands into a TokenStream that represents your SQL query structure. The proc-macro receives this token stream, parses it using syn to build an internal representation of your DSL, and then outputs a new TokenStream with additional tokens or modified tokens.
So the macro gets the tokens from my input, processes them internally, and returns transformed tokens? Is that right?

Exactly. The proc-macro receives TokenStream as input, parses it to extract meaningful data, performs your DSL logic, then constructs a new TokenStream with the result and returns it via the trait method.
One more thing - I noticed my code has a syntax error. The compiler says 'unexpected token' when parsing the input tokens. What's wrong?

You're probably using the wrong parser or not handling whitespace correctly. Make sure you're using syn::parse from the correct version and that your DSL grammar matches what users will actually type into sql!{}. Also check if you're consuming all tokens properly.
